scenario.title: The Single Flip
scenario.narrative: A secure lab door failed, letting a spy inside. The logic: Has_Badge AND Fingerprint_Match. The defense submits 2 test logs and says 'The door was thoroughly tested!' You must prove their tests are legally invalid.

claim: Test 1: Badge=True, Finger=True (Door Opened). Test 2: Badge=False, Finger=False (Door Closed). Our testing is flawless!
charges: The defense claims they proved the Fingerprint scanner works.
However, their 'failing' test changed BOTH the badge and the fingerprint.
Form an objection to show why this invalidates their proof of independent effect.
correct_answer_explanation: Debrief: You shattered their defense! By changing multiple variables, they masked the fingerprint scanner's failure. Welcome to the rule of the 'Single Flip'. Note: In this mission, 'only one condition changes' is an easy beginner-friendly way to introduce MC/DC. In deeper logic, we will look at 'independent effect' even if non-essential things change.
wrong_answer_explanation: That objection doesn't hit the mathematical flaw. Focus on how many variables they flipped at once.
hints: Judge's Note: Look at the defense's logs. Did they change just the fingerprint, or did they swap the badge too?
A valid MC/DC pair must flip EXACTLY ONE variable to prove that specific variable caused the outcome change.

misconceptions.explanation_md: To prove independent effect, you must hold all other variables constant while flipping the target variable. If you change two things at once, you cannot prove which one caused the decision to change.
